annotations.text: Respiratory distress observed shortly after birth in large full-term infants who are delivered by cesarean section is frequently a result of transient tachypnea of the newborn.
This condition is believed to be due to delayed resorption of fetal lung fluid. It usually resolves quickly.
I suspect that transient tachypnea of the newborn prompted the initial x-ray film of the chest, which demonstrated the mass.
Therefore, we are probably dealing with an incidental finding.
The discovery at birth of a mass that proved to be relatively quiescent for the first 17 weeks of life leads me to consider a differential diagnosis of neoplastic and congenital lesions.
Neuroblastomas, sarcomas, teratomas, and leukemia are those most frequently described.
I shall therefore focus on neurogenic tumors of the posterior mediastinum as the primary neoplastic consideration in this infant.
Between 10 and 20 percent of these tumors occur in the mediastinum.
Multiple developmental abnormalities can produce mass lesions in the lower portion of the chest.
Esophageal duplications and neurenteric cysts are largely eliminated in this case on the basis of the radiographic studies.
Other considerations include herniated abdominal contents, bronchogenic cysts, cystic adenomatoid malformations of the lungs, and bronchopulmonary sequestrations.
the solid, or Type III, form of congenital cystic adenomatoid malformation needs to be considered.
Occasional reports of a systemic blood supply to this malformation can cause confusion with the next consideration, pulmonary sequestration.
The extralobar form of pulmonary sequestration is invested in its own pleura and has been considered an accessory lung or a Rokitansky lobe.
I would have obtained an assay for urinary catecholamine values and additional imaging to delineate the vascular features, and I would have proceeded with an exploratory thoracotomy.
I wondered whether the infant might have had hypertension caused by a functioning neuroendocrine tumor.
The discovery at birth of a mass that proved to be relatively quiescent for the first 17 weeks of life leads me to consider a differential diagnosis of neoplastic and congenital lesions.
Thrombocytosis has been reported in association with neural-crest tumors as well as a variety of other childhood neoplasms.
Secondary or reactive thrombocytosis, however, is also associated with inflammatory diseases, drugs, immune disorders, hematologic disorders, asplenia, and stressful states in general.
Beyond these observations the laboratory data available from the case record are of little help regarding neoplasms.
In my judgment a study that should be performed in this setting is a urine assay for catecholamines.

I believed that statistically the diagnosis was most likely to be a left-lower-lobe pulmonary sequestration, but I was troubled by the observations of agitation and bradycardia (80 beats per minute) — an unusual combination.
I therefore largely discarded that information in my analysis of this case.
